%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# Discrete-Time Complex Exponential \(e^{j\Omega n}\)

> **Section Objective:** Learn how the discrete-time complex exponential \(e^{j\Omega n}\) becomes cosine plus sine and how it appears on the complex plane.

---

**Concepts In This Section**

- Euler form of \(e^{j\Omega n}\)
- Frequency \(|\Omega|\)
- Unit-circle magnitude and angle
- Sign and periodicity traps

## 1. Euler’s formula turns \(e^{j\Omega n}\) into cosine plus sine

A discrete-time complex exponential is not a mysterious new waveform. It packages two ordinary real sinusoids into one compact expression.

The **real part** is \(\cos(\Omega n)\) and the **imaginary-axis component** is \(\sin(\Omega n)\). Flipping the sign of the exponent keeps the cosine unchanged but flips the sign of the sine component.

#### Minimal Example

At \(n = 0\): both \(e^{j\Omega \cdot 0} = 1\) and \(e^{-j\Omega \cdot 0} = 1\). The two expressions agree at the starting sample because \(\cos(0) = 1\) and \(\sin(0) = 0\).

## 2. Frequency is \(|\Omega|\), not the sign of the exponent

This is the core definition for this section.

- \(\Omega\) — angular frequency in **radians/sample**
- \(n\) — integer sample index (\(n = \ldots, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, \ldots\))
- \(j\) — the vertical-axis marker in the complex plane (\(j^2 = -1\))

**When to use it:** Any time an exam asks for the real part, imaginary part, cosine/sine expansion, or a comparison of \(e^{j\Omega n}\) versus \(e^{-j\Omega n}\).

#### COMMON MISUSE

The negative exponent does **not** change the frequency. It changes the rotation direction and flips the sign of the sine term. The frequency magnitude \(|\Omega|\) is identical for both forms.

$$e^{j\Omega n} = \cos(\Omega n) + j\sin(\Omega n), \quad e^{-j\Omega n} = \cos(\Omega n) - j\sin(\Omega n)$$


## 3. On the complex plane, \(e^{j\Omega n}\) lives on the unit circle

Both \(e^{j\Omega n}\) and \(e^{-j\Omega n}\) have the same angular frequency magnitude: \(|\Omega|\) radians/sample.

What the sign **does** control is the **direction of rotation** on the complex plane:

- Positive exponent \(+j\Omega n\) → counterclockwise rotation as \(n\) increases
- Negative exponent \(-j\Omega n\) → clockwise rotation as \(n\) increases

#### Minimal Example

\(e^{j(\pi/4)n}\) and \(e^{-j(\pi/4)n}\) both have frequency \(\pi/4\) radians/sample. They trace the same set of angles in opposite order.

#### EXAM TRIGGER

If the problem asks for **frequency** and gives a signed exponent, report the **magnitude** \(|\Omega|\) unless direction is explicitly requested.

#### COMMON MISUSE

Answering \(-\Omega\) as the frequency of \(e^{-j\Omega n}\) is wrong. Frequency is never negative.

$$\text{frequency of } e^{j\Omega n} \text{ and } e^{-j\Omega n} = |\Omega|$$

## 3. On the Complex Plane, \(e^{j\Omega n}\) Lives on the Unit Circle

This identity reveals two facts simultaneously:

- **Magnitude:** \(r = 1\) always. The point never moves away from the unit circle, regardless of \(n\) or \(\Omega\).
- **Angle:** \(\theta = n\Omega\). Each new sample advances the angle by \(\Omega\) radians.

Here \(r\) is the distance from the origin and \(\theta\) is the angle measured counterclockwise from the positive real axis.

**When to use it:** Any exam question asking for magnitude, phase, or complex-plane location of \(e^{j\Omega n}\).

#### Minimal Example

If \(\Omega = \pi/3\) and \(n = 2\), then \(\theta = 2\pi/3\). The point is at angle \(120^\circ\) on the unit circle — still at radius 1.

#### COMMON MISUSE

Thinking the magnitude grows because the expression is called an **exponential**. The word refers to the mathematical form, not growth. The magnitude is always exactly 1.

$$e^{j\Omega n} = re^{j\theta} \quad (r=1,\; \theta=n\Omega)$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="example_support" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- \(e^{j\Omega n} = \cos(\Omega n) + j\sin(\Omega n)\) and \(e^{-j\Omega n} = \cos(\Omega n) - j\sin(\Omega n)\): real part is cosine, imaginary component is sine.
- Frequency of both \(e^{j\Omega n}\) and \(e^{-j\Omega n}\) equals \(|\Omega|\) radians/sample; the sign changes rotation direction, not frequency magnitude.
- \(e^{j\Omega n} = re^{j\theta}\) with \(r = 1\) and \(\theta = n\Omega\): the point always stays on the unit circle; \(n\) advances the angle, not the radius.
- Trap: the negative exponent rotates clockwise and flips the sine sign — it does not change \(|\Omega|\) or move the point off the unit circle.

- Core formula: \(e^{j\Omega n} \text{ and } e^{-j\Omega n} = |\Omega|\).
- Core formula: \(j^2 = -1\).
*In the next section we will use discrete-time signals as inputs to systems.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-quiz-plan" data-quiz-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
